_title: IEC 60364-5-52 — Wiring System Reference Methods for Cable Rating Selection
_note: The reference method determines which column of Annex B current rating tables to use. Choose the method that best describes the ACTUAL installation — not the most favourable method. If in doubt, use the more conservative method.

method_A1.description: Insulated single-core conductors (not multi-core cable) in conduit embedded in a thermally insulating wall or ceiling
method_A1.condition: Conduit surrounded by thermally insulating material (mineral wool, polystyrene, PIR foam) with no air circulation around the cable
method_A1.rating_characteristic: Lowest of all methods — heat cannot dissipate through the insulating material
typical_applications: Single-core PVC cables in conduit passing through insulated stud partitions
Conduit embedded in EPS-insulated wall panels
Any cable fully surrounded by building thermal insulation
design_notes: Avoid where possible — the thermal penalty is severe (ratings are ~25% lower than Method B2 for the same cable)
XLPE cable is strongly preferred over PVC where this method applies — better thermal tolerance
For runs through insulated walls, use the Method A1/A2 rating for the length within insulation, not the overall circuit rating

method_A2.description: Multi-core cable in conduit embedded in a thermally insulating wall
method_A2.condition: Same as A1 but multi-core cable (all conductors in one cable, all within the same conduit in insulation)
method_A2.rating_characteristic: Similar to A1 — slightly lower in some sizes due to multi-core mutual heating
typical_applications: Multi-core flat or round cable in conduit within insulated cavity walls
Cable runs through insulated loft flooring

method_B1.description: Insulated single-core conductors in conduit on a wall or ceiling surface, or in open or ventilated trunking on a wall
method_B1.condition: Conduit or trunking is surface-mounted on the wall — air circulation exists around the outside of the conduit. The conduit itself is the wiring system (not embedded in structure).
method_B1.rating_characteristic: Moderate — better than A1/A2 because air can circulate around the conduit
typical_applications: Single-core conductors in steel or PVC conduit on concrete walls in industrial buildings
PVC-covered single conductors in surface trunking (cable duct)
Single-core cables in ventilated cable trunking
method_B1.table_reference: Annex B Table B.52.3 or B.52.4 (method B1 column)

method_B2.description: Multi-core cable in conduit on a wall or ceiling, or in surface-mounted trunking
method_B2.condition: Multi-core cable within a conduit or trunking that is surface-mounted (not embedded). Air can circulate around the outside of the system.
method_B2.rating_characteristic: Standard moderate rating — most commonly referenced starting point for commercial building final circuits
typical_applications: 2.5mm² 3-core flat cable in PVC conduit on plasterboard ceiling — commercial office lighting
4-core SWA cable in surface conduit in plant room
Multi-core cable in PVC trunking on wall in corridor
General commercial and light industrial final circuit wiring
method_B2.table_reference: Annex B Table B.52.2 (columns for B1/B2)
design_notes: Very commonly used as the reference method for conduit/trunking systems in buildings
Where multiple cables share a conduit or trunking, apply grouping factor Cg (see correction factors)

method_C.description: Single-core or multi-core cable clipped directly to a surface (wall, ceiling, cable tray touching a wall, or under a floor)
method_C.condition: Cable is in direct contact with the mounting surface on one side, with free air on the other three sides. No conduit or trunking.
method_C.rating_characteristic: Higher than B1/B2 because the cable is directly on the surface — better heat transfer to wall AND convection on exposed sides
typical_applications: SWA armoured cable cleated directly to concrete wall in plant room
Non-armoured multi-core cable clipped to wall with cable clips
Cables on perforated tray that is pressed against the wall (not free-standing)
T&E cable clipped to timber joist in floor void (clipped to timber surface)
method_C.table_reference: Annex B Table B.52.4 (Method C column) or B.52.5 for single-core
design_notes: Reference method C gives good ratings — approximately 10–20% higher than B2 for the same cable size
If cables are bunched/grouped on the surface, apply Cg from correction factors table
XLPE armoured cable clipped direct is the standard design for submains in commercial buildings

method_D1.description: Multi-core cable installed in a conduit, duct, or pipe buried in the ground
method_D1.condition: Multi-core cable within a dedicated conduit or duct (typically HDPE or vitrified clay) that is buried. Soil surrounds the conduit. Reference conditions: soil resistivity 0.9 K·m/W, ambient soil 20°C, depth 0.5m.
method_D1.rating_characteristic: Good — soil is a reasonable thermal conductor and provides heat sink. Ratings are often higher than B2 for larger cable sizes.
typical_applications: Power and data cables in HDPE conduits under roadways and paved areas
Service entries to buildings in concrete duct banks
Distribution cables in duct systems between buildings
Cables under car parks in multiple duct runs
correction_factors_required: Cs (soil thermal resistivity if not 0.9 K·m/W)
design_notes: Preferable to D2 (direct buried) for cables that may need future replacement
Multiple cables in same duct — treat as single bundle, apply Cg
Apply soil thermal resistivity correction factor — vary greatly from 0.5 (wet clay) to 3.0 (dry rock)

method_D2.description: Multi-core cable direct buried in ground (no duct or conduit)
method_D2.condition: Armoured cable (SWA or AWA) directly surrounded by soil — no air gap, no duct. Reference conditions: soil resistivity 1.0 K·m/W, ambient soil 20°C, depth 0.7m.
method_D2.rating_characteristic: Slightly higher than D1 because there is no air gap between cable and soil — better thermal contact. But requires armoured cable for mechanical protection.
typical_applications: SWA armoured underground distribution cables between buildings
Underground service entries in gardens and soft ground
Street lighting underground supplies (direct buried in soft verge)
Underground rural distribution cables
method_D2.table_reference: Annex B Table B.52.6 (D2 column) or B.52.7 for armoured
correction_factors_required: Cs (soil thermal resistivity if not 1.0 K·m/W)
design_notes: Cable MUST be suitable for direct burial — armoured or with UV-stable sheath
Minimum burial depth: 600mm under gardens and light trafficking, 900mm under light roads, 1000mm under heavy roads
Marker tape 150mm above cable mandatory
Soil conditions dominate sizing — always assess or assume worst-case resistivity

method_E.description: Multi-core cable on a perforated cable tray, cable ladder, or cleated on stands in free air
method_E.condition: Cable is on a tray or ladder that is mounted away from any surface — free air circulation on all sides. Tray must be perforated (holes > 30% of area) or ladder type. Not applicable to solid-bottom trays (use Method C).
method_E.rating_characteristic: High — free air cooling on all sides gives the best ratings for above-ground multi-core installation
typical_applications: XLPE multi-core armoured cables on cable ladders in main switchrooms
Cables on perforated trays in raised access floors
Cables on cable tray above suspended ceilings (tray not touching ceiling — free air)
Armoured cables on standoff brackets in substation cable areas
method_E.table_reference: Annex B Table B.52.9 (Method E column)
correction_factors_required: Cg (multiple cables — apply when cables are touching or close)
Multi-layer correction (0.80 for 2 layers, 0.73 for 3 layers)
design_notes: Highest practical rating for multi-core cables above ground — specify Method E wherever layout permits
Solid-bottom trays do not qualify as Method E — they restrict underside airflow. Use Method C ratings for solid-bottom trays
When loading a tray, maintain single-layer where possible. Multi-layer requires additional derating.

method_F.description: Single-core cables (three or four) in free air — trefoil formation or flat formation with cables touching
method_F.condition: Individual single-core cables (not a multi-core cable) installed in groups of 3 or 4, in trefoil or flat touching formation, in free air with no nearby surfaces. Typically used for large submains where multi-core cables are impractical.
method_F.rating_characteristic: High — similar to Method E for large cables. Some mutual heating between cables reduces rating slightly vs Method G (spaced).
typical_applications: Large copper or aluminium submains (95mm² to 630mm²) on cable ladders
Generator cable from gen-set to ATS and main switchboard
Transformer LV connections
Large motor feeder cables
method_F.table_reference: Annex B Table B.52.10 (Method F — trefoil) or B.52.11 (flat touching)
design_notes: Use trefoil formation where possible — slightly lower rating but lower inductive reactance and more consistent heat distribution
For single-core cables > 50mm², provide cleats at intervals to maintain formation and prevent cable movement under fault conditions
Factor in the reactive component of impedance for voltage drop calculations (X ≈ 0.07–0.10 mΩ/m per conductor for trefoil)

method_G.description: Single-core cables in free air — flat formation, spaced at least one cable diameter apart (one De spacing between adjacent cable surfaces)
method_G.condition: Individual single-core cables spaced apart in flat formation — air gap between cables allows individual cooling. Requires positive spacing maintenance (cable cleats at intervals).
method_G.rating_characteristic: Highest of all methods — maximum air cooling, no mutual heating between cables when properly spaced
typical_applications: Very large submains or bus wires in main switchrooms where maximum rating is required
Transformer connections where space permits proper spacing
High current generator cables
design_notes: In practice, cables drift together under gravity — use cable cleats at close intervals to maintain spacing
Economic benefit of Method G vs F: approximately 5–10% higher rating — often not worth the added complexity and cleat cost for typical submains
Use Method F ratings for conservative design unless spacing can be guaranteed to be maintained throughout the installation's life

selection_guide._title: Quick reference — choosing the correct reference method

decision_tree.condition: Cable in conduit/trunking AND embedded in thermal insulation wall
decision_tree.method: A1 or A2 — use A2 for multi-core

decision_tree.condition: Cable in conduit/trunking AND surface-mounted on wall or ceiling
decision_tree.method: B1 or B2 — use B2 for multi-core

decision_tree.condition: Multi-core cable clipped directly to wall/ceiling without conduit
Single-core cables clipped to wall (no conduit)
Multi-core cable in buried conduit or duct
Multi-core cable on perforated tray or cable ladder, free air
Single-core cables in free air, trefoil or touching flat
Single-core cables in free air, spaced ≥ 1 diameter apart
Cable in enclosed cable duct (solid walls, limited airflow, surface mounted)
decision_tree.method: B2 — treat as conduit on wall

common_mistakes: Using Method E for cables on solid-bottom trays — must use Method C
Using Method C for cables packed tightly together on a wall — apply grouping factor Cg
Using Method D2 for cables in a duct — use D1
Ignoring Method A1/A2 when a conduit passes through insulated wall sections — even a 0.5m length must be checked
